questions.questionMd: How does a biword index handle a three-word phrase query such as "machine learning model"?

options.text: It stores the entire three-word phrase as a single compound dictionary entry
It decomposes the phrase into overlapping biwords and executes an AND query over machine_learning and learning_model
It falls back to a standard Boolean AND query over the three individual terms
It uses the positional index for any phrase longer than two words

questions.explanationMd: A biword index only stores two-word combinations. To handle longer phrases, the system breaks the query into overlapping pairs (biwords) and intersects their postings lists using an AND merge.

questions.questionMd: A biword index speeds up two-word phrase queries, but which weakness makes it unsuitable for general-purpose phrase retrieval?

options.text: Position information is discarded, making proximity queries impossible
All phrase queries must be preprocessed into single tokens at index build time
Longer phrase queries can produce false positive results and the index size roughly doubles
It cannot support trailing wildcard patterns alongside phrase queries

questions.explanationMd: Because longer phrases are broken down into overlapping biwords (e.g., A B AND B C), the query might falsely match a document containing 'A B' and later 'B C', but not consecutively. Additionally, storing all possible word pairs severely inflates the vocabulary size.

questions.questionMd: In the complete Boolean query engine described in the lecture, which component is responsible for processing the user's query string immediately before the wildcard resolver?

options.text: The query optimizer, which reorders AND operands by ascending document frequency
The spelling corrector, which substitutes misspelled tokens with vocabulary candidates
The Boolean executor, which performs AND, OR, and NOT postings merges
The query parser, which identifies operators, phrases, and wildcard patterns in the raw input

questions.explanationMd: The query parser takes the raw string input and identifies the structural elements (like wildcards, phrases, and Boolean operators) so that specific components like the wildcard resolver can process their respective pieces.

questions.questionMd: A term has a document frequency (DF) of 500 and a term frequency (TF) of 12 in one particular document. What does the DF value of 500 indicate?

options.text: The term appears in 500 different documents in the corpus
The term's postings list contains 500 position entries
The term appears in documents with an average length of 500 tokens
The term appears 500 times across all documents combined

questions.explanationMd: Document Frequency (DF) is explicitly defined as the total number of distinct documents within a corpus that contain the term at least once, regardless of how many times the term appears inside those individual documents.

questions.questionMd: A Boolean AND query contains five terms with document frequencies 620, 3400, 8100, 12000, and 880. Which processing order is optimal?
questions.explanationMd: To optimize an AND query, operands should always be evaluated in ascending order of their Document Frequency (DF). This shrinks the intermediate result set as quickly as possible. The sorted order is 620, 880, 3400, 8100, 12000.

questions.questionMd: During an AND merge with skip pointers, under what condition should the pointer in list $P_1$ jump to the skip target rather than stepping forward by one?

options.text: When the value at the skip target position in $P_1$ is strictly less than the current value in $P_2$
When the value at the skip target position in $P_1$ is less than or equal to the current value in $P_2$
When the current value in $P_1$ is greater than the current value in $P_2$
When the skip target is within $\lfloor\sqrt{p}\rfloor$ positions of the current index in $P_1$

questions.explanationMd: A skip is only safe if it doesn't overshoot the target document ID. Therefore, $P_1$ can safely jump to the skip target if the document ID at the skip target is less than or equal to the document ID currently being pointed to in $P_2$.

questions.questionMd: Why is leading a query with a NOT operation alone considered poor practice?

options.text: It requires scanning the entire corpus to find all document IDs not in the negated list
It produces the same result as an AND query but takes longer to parse
It requires building a secondary inverted index from scratch at query time
It can only be applied after an OR merge has been completed

questions.explanationMd: A pure NOT query (e.g., `NOT apple`) conceptually means returning every document in the universe except those containing 'apple'. This forces the system to load the entire dictionary and all documents, which is computationally unfeasible.

questions.questionMd: When the two postings lists being merged with skip pointers have approximately equal lengths, what effect do skip pointers have on performance?

options.text: The merge terminates earlier than the naive case because both lists can skip simultaneously
Skip pointers have no effect at all because they are only stored for lists longer than 10,000 entries
Skip pointers provide the largest speedup because both lists benefit equally from jumping ahead
The overhead of checking skip conditions at each step can make performance marginally worse than the naive merge

questions.explanationMd: Skip pointers are only beneficial when one list can skip over large portions of another. If the lists are dense and similarly sized, the algorithm rarely skips, meaning the CPU cycles spent checking if a skip is valid are wasted, causing a slight performance drop.

questions.questionMd: During a two-pointer AND merge, when the values at both pointers are equal, which pointers should be advanced?

options.text: Only the pointer into the first list
Both pointers must be advanced
Only the pointer into the shorter list
The pointer into the list with the larger next value

questions.explanationMd: In an AND intersection, when both pointers point to the same document ID, you record that ID as a match. Because neither list will contain that ID again (sets are unique), both pointers must be incremented to check the next IDs.

questions.questionMd: Compared to the original forward dictionary, approximately how much larger does the permuterm index grow?

options.text: Roughly 10 times larger
The same size, since rotations are stored as pointers rather than full strings
Roughly 4 to 5 times larger
Roughly 2 times larger

questions.explanationMd: A permuterm index stores every possible circular rotation of a word. A typical English word length yields many rotations, and literature generally asserts the dictionary size inflates by roughly a factor of 10.

questions.questionMd: In the Boolean retrieval model, which operator precedence order is correct when no parentheses are used?

options.text: NOT is evaluated first, then AND, then OR
AND is evaluated first, then NOT, then OR
NOT and AND share equal precedence and are evaluated before OR
OR is evaluated first, then AND, then NOT

questions.explanationMd: Standard Boolean logic precedence generally dictates that NOT has the highest precedence (evaluated first), followed by AND, and finally OR has the lowest precedence.

questions.questionMd: The Soundex algorithm is best described as a tool designed for which specific purpose?

options.text: Phonetic matching of proper names by encoding names that sound similar to the same code
Ranking spelling correction candidates by their edit distance from a misspelled query
General-purpose spelling correction for any English vocabulary term
Wildcard query resolution by hashing vocabulary terms into fixed-length codes

questions.explanationMd: Soundex is specifically a phonetic algorithm meant to group words (originally proper nouns and surnames) that sound alike but are spelled differently (e.g., Smith vs. Smyth).

questions.questionMd: When building a k-gram index for general English text information retrieval, which value of k is described as the recommended tradeoff between index size and retrieval precision?
questions.explanationMd: In general IR literature, a 3-gram (trigram) is recommended for English as it provides enough specificity to reduce false positives without creating an overly bloated index, representing the optimal tradeoff point.

questions.questionMd: In the Boolean retrieval model, what does a query return?

options.text: A ranked list of documents sorted by relevance score
A probability distribution over matching documents
A set of document IDs sorted by term frequency
A set of document IDs with no ordering or relevance scores

questions.explanationMd: The Boolean model is strictly binary: a document either matches the query perfectly, or it doesn't. It does not compute partial matches, ranks, or scores. It simply returns an unranked set of matching document IDs.

questions.questionMd: Using a permuterm index, how is the wildcard query "*tion" translated before performing a dictionary lookup?

options.text: It is reversed to noit and a suffix scan is performed on the dictionary
It is decomposed into k-grams and an intersection query is run against the k-gram index
It is padded to stions and an exact match is performed in the permuterm dictionary
It is rotated to tion$ and then a prefix lookup is performed on tion$

questions.explanationMd: To handle a wildcard query in a permuterm index, an end-of-word symbol `$` is appended (resulting in `*tion$`). Then, the term is rotated so the wildcard `*` is at the end (resulting in `tion$*`). The system then performs a standard prefix lookup on `tion$`.

questions.questionMd: In the positional index approach to phrase queries, what is the role of the first phase?

options.text: It verifies that query terms appear at consecutive positions in each document
It resolves wildcard patterns into concrete vocabulary terms using the k-gram index
It applies Jaccard similarity to rank candidate documents by term overlap
It uses an AND merge to produce a candidate set of documents containing all query terms

questions.explanationMd: Checking positions is computationally expensive. Therefore, the first step is always to perform a fast Boolean AND merge to retrieve only the subset of documents that contain all the target words before analyzing their exact positions.

questions.questionMd: Why does the phrase query "machine learning" return fewer results than the Boolean query machine AND learning?

options.text: The phrase query automatically removes stop words, reducing the candidate set before merging
The phrase query restricts matching to document titles, whereas the Boolean AND searches the full text
The phrase query additionally requires the two terms to appear consecutively in the correct order within a document
The phrase query applies a Jaccard similarity threshold that the Boolean AND does not use

questions.explanationMd: A Boolean AND query matches a document if both words appear anywhere in the text, independent of position. A phrase query is far stricter, demanding exact proximity and ordering.

questions.questionMd: In the Soundex phonetic encoding algorithm, how are vowels treated after the initial letter is recorded?

options.text: Vowels are removed entirely and do not contribute a digit code
Vowels are each assigned the digit code 0 and retained in the output
Vowels cause the adjacent consonant codes to be doubled in the output
Vowels are treated identically to the consonants that immediately follow them

questions.explanationMd: After keeping the first letter, Soundex drops all vowels (A, E, I, O, U) as well as H, W, and Y, to collapse phonetic variations into standard consonant-driven codes.

questions.questionMd: What is the correct set of 3-grams (with boundary markers) for the vocabulary term "query"?

options.text: ($qu, que, uer, ery)
($qu, que, uer, ery, ry$)
($qu, que, uer, ry$)
(qu, que, uer, ery, ry)

questions.explanationMd: First, append boundary markers: `$query$`. Then extract sliding sequences of 3 characters: `$qu`, `que`, `uer`, `ery`, `ry$`.

questions.questionMd: The two-pointer AND merge achieves $O(m+n)$ time complexity only because of one crucial property of postings lists. What is that property?

options.text: Postings lists are stored in sorted order of document ID
Postings lists use variable-byte encoded gaps between document IDs
Postings lists contain positional information for each document
Postings lists are stored in order of decreasing term frequency

questions.explanationMd: Because the document IDs are sorted in ascending order, the intersection algorithm can simply walk two pointers linearly through the arrays. If they weren't sorted, checking for intersections would require a far slower $O(m \times n)$ comparison process.

questions.questionMd: Given two sorted postings lists of lengths m and n, what is the time complexity of the two-pointer AND merge algorithm?

options.text: $O(m \times n)$
$O(m \log n)$

questions.explanationMd: By traversing both pre-sorted lists simultaneously, the maximum number of steps required equals the combined length of the two lists, resulting in linear $O(m+n)$ complexity.

questions.questionMd: When two sorted postings lists are merged with OR and both pointers point to the same document ID, what is the correct action?

options.text: Emit the document ID twice to reflect it appears in both lists and advance both pointers
Emit the document ID once and advance only the pointer into the shorter list
Skip the document ID entirely because it indicates an AND match, not an OR match
Emit the document ID once and advance both pointers

questions.explanationMd: An OR operation is a set union. Sets contain unique elements. Therefore, the common document ID should be emitted exactly once to the result list, and both pointers must advance to continue the merge without getting stuck.

questions.questionMd: What is the recommended skip pointer interval for a postings list of length 400?
questions.explanationMd: A standard heuristic dictates the skip frequency should be $\sqrt{P}$, where $P$ is the length of the list. Since $\sqrt{400} = 20$, the interval is 20.

questions.questionMd: When optimizing a multi-term AND query by sorting operands in order of ascending document frequency, what is the primary benefit?

options.text: Skip pointers become unnecessary for the remaining operations
The total number of postings lists loaded into memory is reduced
The OR merge step is eliminated from the query plan
Intermediate result sets shrink fastest when processing begins with the rarest terms

questions.explanationMd: By starting intersections with the smallest sets, the size of the intermediate sets shrinks rapidly. Processing a massive set early would consume significant memory and CPU, only for most of it to be discarded later.

questions.questionMd: The k-gram index built over vocabulary terms serves two distinct retrieval functions. Which pair correctly identifies both?

options.text: Biword index construction and permuterm rotation lookup
Wildcard query resolution and spelling correction candidate retrieval
Phrase query position verification and proximity query distance checking
Query optimization by document frequency and skip pointer construction

questions.explanationMd: The k-gram index is a versatile tool used both to resolve wildcards (e.g., finding all words containing 'red') and to rapidly retrieve a wide net of candidate terms that share substrings with a misspelled word for spelling correction.

questions.questionMd: Embedding skip pointers into a postings list at index build time introduces which tradeoff?

options.text: Postings lists can no longer be gap-encoded because skip targets violate sorted order
The index occupies more storage space in exchange for faster AND merge performance
Compression of postings lists becomes impossible once skip pointers are inserted
The index must be rebuilt from scratch each time a new document is added to the corpus

questions.explanationMd: Skip pointers augment the lists with extra data (the target IDs and lengths), inflating the index size on disk. However, this extra storage buys significant execution speed during long AND queries by allowing bypasses.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following is a fundamental limitation of the Boolean retrieval model?

options.text: Query processing time scales quadratically with the number of query terms
A document that satisfies all but one query term is excluded entirely from results
The model cannot support the NOT operator on large corpora
Results cannot be cached because they change with each query

questions.explanationMd: Because Boolean logic is rigidly binary (match vs non-match), a document that perfectly satisfies an information need but happens to be missing one specific keyword in an AND query will be brutally omitted. It lacks graded relevance.

questions.questionMd: Which sequence correctly describes the three-stage spelling correction pipeline?

options.text: Edit distance ranking → Jaccard similarity filtering → k-gram candidate retrieval
K-gram candidate retrieval → edit distance ranking → Jaccard similarity filtering
Jaccard similarity filtering → k-gram candidate retrieval → edit distance ranking
K-gram candidate retrieval → Jaccard similarity filtering → edit distance ranking

questions.explanationMd: First, retrieve all candidates sharing k-grams (fast but noisy). Second, filter out the worst matches using Jaccard string similarity (cheap heuristic). Finally, rank the surviving candidates using exact Levenshtein edit distance (expensive but accurate).

questions.questionMd: Which query structure correctly follows the best practice for using the NOT operator?

options.text: Apply NOT to the largest postings list to maximize the pruning effect
Replace every NOT operand with an equivalent OR over all other terms
Perform an AND merge first to reduce the candidate set, then apply NOT to the result
Lead the query with NOT to eliminate irrelevant documents before any AND merge

questions.explanationMd: Computing a pure NOT is extremely expensive because it targets the entire universe of documents. The standard best practice is to resolve positive AND/OR constraints first to build a small candidate set, then filter that restricted set using the NOT condition.

questions.questionMd: A spelling corrector using a Jaccard threshold of 0.2 consistently returns no candidates for misspelled words of four characters or fewer. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Short words produce very few k-grams, so Jaccard scores are inherently lower and often fall below the fixed threshold
Edit distance computation fails on short strings because the dynamic programming table is too small
The k-gram index only indexes vocabulary terms of five characters or more by default
Short words are automatically classified as stop words and excluded from the k-gram index

questions.explanationMd: Jaccard relies on the ratio of overlap. Short words yield very few k-grams. Even a 1-character typo destroys a large proportion of these k-grams, causing the Jaccard coefficient to drop sharply, often rendering static thresholds (like 0.2) ineffective.

questions.questionMd: For the phrase query "A B C" evaluated against a positional index, which position relationship must hold in a matching document?

options.text: A must appear before B, and B must appear before C, with no constraint on the exact gap
A and C must appear at adjacent positions, with B appearing anywhere in the document
A must appear at some position p, B at position $p+1$, and C at position $p+2$
A, B, and C must each appear at least once and their positions must sum to a value below a threshold

questions.explanationMd: A true phrase query demands exact, gapless sequence. If the first term is at position `p`, the next term must be exactly one spot over (`p+1`), and the next exactly one spot after that (`p+2`).

questions.questionMd: A proximity query is written as `term1 /k term2`. Which condition must hold in a candidate document for it to be included in the result?

options.text: The absolute difference between any occurrence position of term1 and any occurrence position of term2 must be at most k
term1 must appear exactly k positions before term2 in every occurrence
The total count of term1 and term2 occurrences in the document must equal k
The terms must appear in the same sentence and within k paragraphs of each other

questions.explanationMd: The proximity operator `/k` specifies a maximum acceptable distance between two terms. They can occur in any order, provided the absolute difference between their positions is less than or equal to `k`.

questions.questionMd: What is the Levenshtein edit distance between "information" and "informtion"?
questions.explanationMd: The string "informtion" is missing the letter 'a'. It requires exactly one operation (an insertion of 'a') to transform it back to "information". Therefore, the edit distance is 1.

questions.questionMd: After intersecting k-gram postings to resolve a wildcard query, why is a mandatory post-filtering step required?

options.text: K-gram postings lists are unsorted, so the intersection result contains duplicate terms
Post-filtering converts k-gram integer IDs back into human-readable vocabulary terms
The k-gram intersection can only retrieve terms from a single partition of the vocabulary at a time
K-gram matching is a necessary but not sufficient condition—candidate terms may not actually match the original wildcard pattern

questions.explanationMd: A word might contain all the required k-grams of a query but still fail to match the overall pattern. For example, evaluating 'red*' via k-grams might falsely match the word 'ordered' because it contains 'red', even though it doesn't start with it.

questions.questionMd: Under which condition do skip pointers provide the greatest reduction in comparisons during an AND merge?

options.text: When both postings lists have identical length
When the query contains three or more AND operands
When both postings lists contain more than 10,000 entries
When the two postings lists are very unequal in length

questions.explanationMd: Skip pointers are designed to fast-forward through blocks of document IDs. This only happens frequently when merging a very short list with a very long list, allowing the shorter list to "skip" over huge irrelevant swaths of the longer one.

questions.questionMd: Levenshtein edit distance counts the minimum number of operations needed to transform one string into another. Which set of operations does it use?

options.text: Split a string, merge two strings, substitute one character for another
Insert a character, delete a character, transpose two adjacent characters
Substitute one character for another, transpose two adjacent characters, delete a character
Insert a character, delete a character, substitute one character for another

questions.explanationMd: Classic Levenshtein distance recognizes exactly three operations: insertion, deletion, and substitution. Transpositions are specifically handled by the modified Damerau-Levenshtein variant.

questions.questionMd: Given two k-gram sets A and B, which expression correctly computes their Jaccard similarity?

options.text: $|A \cap B| / |A|$
$|A \cup B| / |A \cap B|$
$|A \cap B| / |A \cup B|$

questions.explanationMd: Jaccard similarity evaluates the overlap between two sets. The formula mathematically divides the size of the intersection (shared elements) by the size of the union (total unique elements).

questions.questionMd: When performing an OR merge on two sorted postings lists of lengths m and n, which statement correctly describes the size of the result?

options.text: The result is always smaller than both input lists
The result is always exactly $m + n$
The result is always equal to the shorter list's length
The result is always at least as large as the longer of the two input lists

questions.explanationMd: An OR query is a set union. At a minimum, the final list will contain every item from the longer list. The final size will be anywhere from $max(m,n)$ (if one list is a subset of the other) up to $m+n$ (if they share no document IDs).

questions.questionMd: In the three-stage spelling correction pipeline, what is the primary purpose of the Jaccard similarity filtering stage?

options.text: To rank the final correction suggestions by their phonetic similarity to the misspelled word
To build the initial candidate set by finding all vocabulary terms sharing at least one k-gram
To apply context-sensitive correction by comparing candidates against a bigram language model
To eliminate most implausible candidates cheaply before the expensive edit distance computation

questions.explanationMd: Edit distance is computationally heavy. Jaccard is applied as a fast, cheap heuristic filter to severely narrow down a large candidate pool into a manageable handful of likely words before calculating Levenshtein matrices.
